{"body":"![Image](https://github.com/user-attachments/assets/abdd22e7-10e7-4047-b102-a4d13523798b)

Image From [Mitochondrial protein transport: Versatility of translocases and mechanisms - PubMed](https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/36931257/) 

We have modelled each import pathway based on the start and destination, plus the maturation steps. We have modelled D. melanogaster and fission yeast, and the models are equivalent with very minor differences (eg. MIM complex in yeast is replaced by a functionally analogous system in metazoa).

This is the set of terms required to describe all mitochondrial import pathways:
	
- [x] 1. GO_NEW mitochondrial protein import pathway (is_ mitochondrial transport)
Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol to their final destination in the mitochondria.

- [x] 2. GO_NEW ~mitochondrial outer membrane alpha helical protein insertion~ (1. In the image)
alpha helical protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
Narrow synonym MIM pathway (different complex in higher eukaryotes)
Narrow synonym: MTCH pathway
Definition: The import of alpha helical and tail-anchored proteins from the cytosol and their insertion into the mitochondrial outer membrane.
Note:In yeast, the insertase is the mitochondrial import (MIM) complex, composed of Mim1 and Mim2. In higher eukaryotes, the functional counterpart MTCH performs a similar role despite lacking sequence and structural homology, reflecting convergent evolution (PMID:40782022)
 	Model: [[Dmel](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003239)][[Spombe](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69a0c46f00003187)]

- [x]  3.  [GO:0030150](https://www.ebi.ac.uk/QuickGO/term/GO:0030150)    protein import into mitochondrial matrix (2 in the image)
Synonym exact: TOM-TIM23-PAM presequence matrix import pathway
NewDefinition: The import of proteins with mitochond

GO_NEW ~TOM-TIM23-SORT protein import and lateral insertion into the inner membrane~ (2b in the image) TOM-TIM23-SORT-mediated protein insertion into the mitochondrial inner membrane Definition: The import of proteins from the cytosol across the outer mitochondrial membrane and laterally inserted into the inner membrane. All can be annotated to other more appropriate terms - [ ] GO:1903748 negative regulation of protein localization to mitochondrion - [ ] GO:1903747 regulation of protein localization to mitochondrion https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/32107 - [ ] GO:0070096 mitochondrial outer membrane translocase complex assembly Assembly of TOM isn’t a separate assembly process, assembly is only affected by import pathways so the correct way to annotate this would be to add TOM subunits as targets of the appropriate import pathways. https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/32108 ","comments":[{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7wzN0q","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"cc @hattrill ","createdAt":"2026-03-11T15:17:22Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4039957802","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xnrF6","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"@ValWood Looking at oxidative folding dependent IMS import. The major route is via the Mia40 system, but there is also a route where CCS1 acts in place of Mia40, acting on itself and SOD. Reference: PMID:24026195. For SOD, looks like it is also coupled with metal ion loading mediated by CCS1 https://pubmed.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/30530491/ So looks like there are at least two pathways for oxidative-folding dependent IMS transport and probably another that is independent of oxidative folding. Will continue to dig. I think refining the IMS transport target list will help - on that task just now.","createdAt":"2026-03-13T09:04:57Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4053709178","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xp9ly","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" A very brief summary of the yeast and human differences in the Ccs-dependent pathways: In yeast, Ccs1 is first imported via the Mia40/Erv1 disulfide relay system. Resident Ccs1 then recruits incoming Sod1 precursors by oxidative folding. A minor parallel route allows some Sod1 to enter the IMS independently of Ccs1, via Mia40 directly. In humans: CCS1 import is autocatalytic, with mature IMS-resident CCS1 acting as its own import receptor via oxidative folding. This reaction is ROS-sensitive -> mitochondrial oxidative stress directly increases IMS recruitment of both CCS1 and SOD1. Metalation and oxidative folding of Sod1: Sod1 requires copper and a zinc ion cofactors. Ccs1 directly mediates copper delivery to Sod1 and forms a transient intermolecular disulfide with Sod1 to facilitate copper transfer and disulfide bond formation simultaneously. Zinc incorporation is thought to occur independently of Ccs1, prior to the Ccs1 interaction. (this is generalised as still some debated details between human and yeast) ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T11:03:33Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4054309234","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xqQtm","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Could we model is as a single IMS branching pathway post TOM (this would be analogous to the amino acid biosynthesis pathways, we now only represent as a single pathway to get the same result. The GO-CAMs will provide the mechanistic details of how you arrive there. We could then add additional narrow synonyms for specific pathways. ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T11:19:23Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":false,"minimizedReason":"","reactionGroups":[{"content":"THUMBS_UP","users":{"totalCount":1}}],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4054387558","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xsItl","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"Think that pombe is more similar to human than to S.cerevisiae for the ccs pathway. Have modelled it like this for now, with the metal ion chaperone activity part of protein maturation rather than import. [Model](http://noctua.geneontology.org/workbench/noctua-visual-pathway-editor/?model_id=gomodel%3A69b3372b00000249)","createdAt":"2026-03-13T12:52:41Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"resolved","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4054879077","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xtf6A","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"OK, I think yeast is a bit different. The mitochondrial version of superoxide dismutase is in the matrix not the IMS, and it isn't copper dependent (the cytosolic one is)...the mitochondrial one uses manganese as its cofactor ","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:55:48Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055236224","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xtjnz","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"actually claude says Sod1 (Cu/Zn-SOD) — the cytoplasmic form Primarily found in the cytoplasm, though a small fraction can also be present in the mitochondrial intermembrane space but I don't know if this is true for fission yeast where the cytosolic form hasn't been detected in the mitochondria","createdAt":"2026-03-13T13:57:28Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055251443","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xuaHA","author":{"login":"hattrill"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" my claude.ai says: In the mitochondrial IMS specifically, the same chemistry applies. CCS is imported and retained in the IMS (via the Mia40/Erv1 pathway in yeast, or self-mediated import in S. pombe/humans), where it loads Cu(I) into apoSOD1 and simultaneously introduces the intramolecular disulfide, maturing SOD1 for its antioxidant role against respiratory chain-derived superoxide. And suggests:

You may be thinking of SOD2, which is the manganese-containing superoxide dismutase — but that is a different enzyme localised to the mitochondrial matrix, not SOD1.

SOD1 is definitively a Cu/Zn enzyme, in all compartments where it is found — cytosol, nucleus, and mitochondrial IMS. It absolutely requires copper for catalysis (the redox-active metal cycling Cu²⁺/Cu⁺ during dismutation) and zinc for structural stability. CCS is its dedicated copper chaperone.

SOD2 is the Mn-containing enzyme, localised exclusively to the mitochondrial matrix, and is completely unrelated structurally to SOD1. It has no CCS and no copper requirement — manganese is inserted by a different maturation pathway.

So in the context of this conversation:

Enzyme | Metal | Localisation | Chaperone -- | -- | -- | -- SOD1 | Cu + Zn | Cytosol, nucleus, IMS | CCS SOD2 | Mn | Mitochondrial matrix | None (Mn inserted differently)

The distinction matters here because the entire CCS import pathway into the IMS, and the oxidative folding mechanism we've been discussing, is specifically about getting copper to SOD1 — manganese and SOD2 are a completely separate system.

----- But I think I might need to look at the mechanistic differences (and whether to model them), as in the IMS, Cu(I) loading - then disuphide then Cu(I) -> Cu(II)","createdAt":"2026-03-13T14:20:19Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4055474624","viewerDidAuthor":false},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws7xwXRX","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":"OK, I went down a rabbit hole today curating css1 papers. The authors didn't detect it in the IMS space but they didn't rule it out so I'll infer it. I deleted the inferences earlier but I'll add them back... The remaining items (2–7, renames, obsoletions) are not addressed here — let me know when you'd like those tackled. --- 🤖 **Generated by @dragon-ai-agent** - Model: `claude-opus-4-6` - Agent harness: claude-code - Triggered by: @ValWood - Run: [View workflow run](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/actions/runs/23843237567)","createdAt":"2026-04-01T10:16:27Z","includesCreatedEdit":false,"isMinimized":true,"minimizedReason":"outdated","reactionGroups":[],"url":"https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711#issuecomment-4169027451","viewerDidAuthor":true},{"id":"IC_kwDOAkAHws74gc9-","author":{"login":"ValWood"},"authorAssociation":"MEMBER","body":" @dragon-ai-agent please Move GO:0045041 protein import into mitochondrial intermembrane space to be a descendant of mitochondrial protein import pathway Remove all the existing parents parents to this term, because the existing branch will be reviewed and many terms will be obsoleted, or different parents instated later. content.content:
---
name: Obsoletion notice
about: This template is to notify ontology users of a term obsoletion.
title: 'Obsoletion notice: [GO ID: term label]'
labels: obsoletion
assignees: ''

---

Dear all, 

The proposal has been made to obsolete [GO ID: term label]. 
(keep one, remove others): 

* The reason for obsoletion is that the term is not clearly defined and usage has been inconsistent
*  The reason for obsoletion is that the meaning of the term is ambiguous.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that this term represents a GO-CAM model.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that this term represent an assay and not a real GO process. 
* The reason for obsoletion is that this represents a phenotype.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that the data from the paper for which the term was requested can be accurately described using [appropriate GO term] 
* The reason for obsoletion is that this term represents a gene product.
* The reason for obsoletion is that this term refers to a class of proteins.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that this term was added in error.
* The reason for obsoletion is that more specific terms were created.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that this term was an unnecessary grouping term.
* The reason for obsoletion is that is not known to be catalyzed by any gene product.
*  The reason for obsoletion is that it more specific than the specificity of any known gene product.
* The reason for obsoletion is that there is no evidence that this function/process/component exists.
* (or add other reason)

Annotations:
There are X experimental annotations to this term by (list of impacted groups and their respective numbers of EXP and ISS annotations). 
or: There are no experimental annotations to this term.

There are X InterPro2GO/UniRule/UniProtKW mappings to this term. 
or: There are no mappings to this term.

This term is included in the XXX subset(s).
or: This term is not present in any subset.

Annotations can be viewed here: [link to GitHub ticket in the go-annotation tracker].

If obsolete term has a 'replaced by' term: (optional)
This term is being replaced by [GO term] 

If obsolete term has a 'consider' term:  (optional)
Annotations can be transferred to [suggested GO term(s)] 

Any comments can be  added to the issue: [link to GitHub ticket in the go-ontology tracker]. 
Unless objections are received by [DATE], we will assume that you agree to this change.

Thanks, 

The GO-Ontology Editors

id: GO:1903636
name: regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
namespace: biological_process
def: "Any process that modulates the frequency, rate or extent of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane." [GO_REF:0000058, GOC:TermGenie, PMID:16374546]
synonym: "regulation of mitochondrial outer membrane protein import" EXACT [GOC:TermGenie]

intersection_of: GO:0065007 ! biological regulation
intersection_of: regulates GO:0045040 ! protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
created_by: pga

content.text: Now I'll draft the announcement email and post it as a comment on the issue.

input.command:
cat > /tmp/announcement.md << 'EOF'
Draft obsoletion announcement (for posting to the [go-announcements tracker](https://github.com/geneontology/go-announcements/issues/new?template=obsoletion-notice.md)):

---

**Title:** Obsoletion notice: GO:1903636 regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane, GO:1903637 negative regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane, GO:1903638 positive regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane

---

Dear all,

The proposal has been made to obsolete the following terms:

- GO:1903636 regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
- GO:1903637 negative regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane
- GO:1903638 positive regulation of protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane

The reason for obsoletion is that these terms were added in error. They are regulation terms of the parent term GO:0045040 (protein insertion into mitochondrial outer membrane), which is itself being obsoleted as part of the restructuring of mitochondrial protein import (see https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/31711).

There are no experimental annotations to these terms.

There are no InterPro2GO/UniRule/UniProtKW mappings to these terms.

These terms are not present in any subset.

Any comments can be added to the issue: https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/issues/32102.

Thanks,

The GO-Ontology Editors

---
🤖 **Generated by @dragon-ai-agent**
- Model: `claude-opus-4-7`
- Agent harness: claude-code
- Triggered by: @raymond91125
- Run: [View workflow run](https://github.com/geneontology/go-ontology/actions/runs/26479946287)
EOF
gh issue comment 32102 --body-file /tmp/announcement.md

content.text: Posted the draft obsoletion announcement as a comment on issue #32102. The draft follows the [go-announcements obsoletion-notice template](https://github.com/geneontology/go-announcements/blob/master/.github/ISSUE_TEMPLATE/obsoletion-notice.md), keeping only the relevant "added in error" reason line, includes the linkage to the parent term restructuring (#31711), and omits the objections-deadline sentence as requested.
